title: Dyeing with turmeric
subtitle: A bright yellow-gold dye from kitchen turmeric on alum-mordanted wool or silk
excerpt: Turmeric produces a bright, warm yellow-gold on alum-mordanted wool and silk. The colour is fugitive (it fades in sunlight), making it unsuitable for items worn outdoors regularly, but excellent for decorative textiles, samples, and low-light household use.
sourceNotes: Turmeric as a dye is described in Jenny Dean, Wild Colour (1999, in-copyright, substance only referenced), and in historical dyeing documentation in Pellew, Dyes and Dyeing (1913, public domain). The fugitive quality of turmeric dye is widely documented in natural dye literature.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt that forms a chemical bond between the wool fibre and the dye. Alum is the standard mordant for most plant yellows including turmeric.
A dye that fades relatively quickly on exposure to light, washing, or both. Turmeric is a fugitive dye; the yellow colour will shift toward pale cream with extended exposure to direct sunlight.
The pot of water containing the dissolved or extracted dye material into which the mordanted fibre is placed.

content.text: Turmeric is the most accessible natural dye available from a supermarket shelf. It requires a standard alum
and produces an immediate, strong yellow-gold on wool and silk. The drawback is that turmeric is a
: it fades perceptibly in direct sunlight over weeks to months. Use it for items that will live indoors, out of direct light, and the colour will persist well. The
temperature is lower than for most plant dyes because turmeric colour builds quickly even at 60 to 70 degrees Celsius.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Use dedicated dye-pots. Wear gloves and apron. Alum is low-hazard; keep out of reach of food and children.

content.text: Dissolve 15 g alum in hot water and add to the dye-pot with enough cold water to cover the fibre loosely. Add the pre-wetted wool and raise the temperature to 80 degrees Celsius over 30 minutes. Hold for 45 minutes. Allow to cool before removing the fibre.

Dissolve 50 g ground turmeric in 500 ml of warm water, stirring well. Add to a clean dye-pot with enough water to cover the fibre loosely. The dye dissolves easily; no straining is needed but the pot will look very yellow and will stain easily, so use a dedicated pot.

Add the mordanted, still-damp fibre to the turmeric dye bath. Heat slowly to 65 degrees Celsius (no higher; higher temperatures can shift the colour toward orange) and hold for 30 minutes, stirring occasionally. Remove the fibre and rinse in water of the same temperature, then progressively cooler water. The colour will be bright; it will calm slightly once dry.

The alum mordant bath and the turmeric dye bath can be poured down the foul drain or composted in small home quantities. Turmeric is a food-grade spice with no significant environmental hazard at these volumes.

An iron after-bath deepens the yellow toward a warm olive-green. Add 2 g of dissolved iron sulphate to 2 litres of warm water, immerse the rinsed yellow fibre for 5 to 10 minutes, then rinse thoroughly. The iron modifier shifts the yellow-gold perceptibly toward green in a single step.

items.problem: The colour faded very quickly after the first wash
items.cause: The fibre was washed in hot water or left in direct sunlight while damp
items.fix: Turmeric dye is fugitive but stable if handled gently. Always wash in cool water and dry in shade or indoors.

items.problem: The colour is patchy and uneven across the fibre
items.cause: The turmeric was not fully dissolved before adding the fibre, or the fibre was not moved during dyeing
items.fix: Stir the turmeric thoroughly into warm water before adding to the pot, and stir the fibre gently every few minutes during dyeing.
